Mock out the call and response for LoginUtils, so we can
better control how our endpoint will behave in different situations

We'll be mocking requests and responses and so on throughout these
tests, so just initialize them here for later use.  Notice
that we use spy for the LoginServlet.  This specifically allows us to mock
out its loginUtils class, but otherwise, all LoginServlet calls
are to the real code.  Why mock LoginUtils? So that we can prevent
real calls into that class - it's assumed we have already tested
that class on its own, we just want to test code specific to this class.

LoginServlet uses a static method, {@link ServletUtils#forwardToResult}
that has a call to {@link HttpServletRequest#getRequestDispatcher} that
we need to mock out.

These tests cover the testing of the endpoint itself, not so much
anything it calls.  That's why we mock out everything outside
this class.  It makes our tests run very quickly, and enables us to
focus with laser-like precision on the functionality this class
provides.  Other tests will provide integration testing - but not
this one.

All these random values should have sufficient entropy

passwords generated using the posix command line:

comments:
   generate random printing text    12 chars  1000 lines   replace backslash      replace double-quote        put quote           put quote              put comma     save to file
                                                          with escaped backslash      with escaped           at beginning           at end                at end
command:
tr -cd [:graph:] < /dev/urandom | fold -w12 | head -n1000 | sed 's:\\:\\\\:g'       | sed 's:":\\":g'       | sed 's:^:":' |    sed 's:$:":'      |     sed 's:$:,:' > better_passwords.txt

the :graph: option says to print all graphical characters - anything you could easily type on a keyboard
the :alnum: option keeps it to characters and numbers

Results:
of the 10-character passwords, these failed:
ll5acnE``u
`/B88{lE4n
yOHG%EY0u0
qhhPIAN0?y

All the 12 and 14-character passwords passed muster

Testing of the framework we use for password complexity analysis.

The idea with this framework is that it measures how good the password is
outside of needing this many capital letters, this many symbols, etc.

Instead, it is supposed to simply look at the complexity and decide whether
the password is sufficient.

Testing of the framework we use for password complexity analysis.

The idea with this framework is that it measures how good the password is
outside of needing this many capital letters, this many symbols, etc.

Instead, it is supposed to simply look at the complexity and decide whether
the password is sufficient.

A business process to evaluate an attempt to register a new user.
  1. Make sure the username and password aren't null or empty
  2. Make sure the user isn't already in the database
  3. Check if the password is sufficiently complex to be secure
  4. Create a new account for this user, saving the user's credentials

A business process to evaluate an attempt to register a new user.
  1. Make sure the username and password aren't null or empty
  2. Make sure the user isn't already in the database
  3. Check if the password is sufficiently complex to be secure
  4. Create a new account for this user, saving the user's credentials

Whether we qualify a password as good.

To summarize - we check the following here:

  1. The password must not be empty
  2. it must not be too short (less than 6 characters)
  3. it cannot be too long (more than 100 characters - this is because the framework
  4. analysis slows to a crawl with more than 100 characters)
  5. Whether the entropy framework we're using considers the password good.

See {@link PasswordResult}

Determine the first date which this person is able to obtain a driver's license, per Virginia's law that they be 16 years and 3 months.

calculates using this method:

birthDate.plusYears(16).plusMonths(3);

Determine the first date which this person is able to obtain a driver's license, per Virginia's law that they be 16 years and 3 months.

calculates using this method:

birthDate.plusYears(16).plusMonths(3);

In Virginia, the law states you must be at least 16 years and 3 months
old to get a drivers license.

In this test we'll presume a straightforward happy path.  A person
is born Jan 1, 1990.  When do they get their driver's license?

1990 + 16 years = 2006
Jan + 3 months = April
So, April 1, 2006.
How many days is that from the day they were born?

In Virginia, the law states you must be at least 16 years and 3 months
old to get a drivers license.

Delete a borrower from the database

Search for book loans by borrower

A borrower may have more than one loan.  But a book
can only be loaned to one borrower.  That is why when we
search for loans by borrower, we may get multiple loans
back, but when we search by book, we only get one back.
A book cannot be loaned to two people at the same time!

==========================================================
==========================================================

 Micro ORM
   Demo has a simplistic Object Relational Mapper (ORM)
   implementation.  These are the methods that comprise
   the mechanisms for that.

   In comparison, a gargantuan project like Hibernate
   would consist of a heckuva-lot-more than this.  That's
   why this one is termed, "micro"

==========================================================
==========================================================

Creates an object that is used to avoid some of the boilerplate
in running database CRUD operations.

This class encapsulates some of the actions related to injecting data into a prepared SQL statement, so that we are able to summarize what we want done without all the annoying boilerplate. See examples like {@link PersistenceLayer#saveNewBorrower}

Was necessary to suppress the nullness warnings on this class due to its use of generics. The generic R is the result type - if we ask for a string, R would be a String. On the other hand if R might be a compound type, like Employee.

Under lending, books and borrowers inputs have three modes.

b) If 1 - 9, show a dropdown
c) If 10 and up, show an autocomplete
